refined_fr.refined_fr: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a numerical availability target, and (2) doing so within a defined measurement window. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent design decisions and verification strategies. The refined FR focuses on the primary functional obligation—sustaining availability performance—while treating the measurement period as a constraint context rather than a functional requirement. This formulation is solution-neutral: it does not prescribe redundancy, predictive maintenance, or any specific DP; it only states the availability outcome required.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total time − unplanned downtime) / total time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold at the end of the evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: Maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: Establish and communicate the availability measurement methodology and evaluation period to all stakeholders.
FR-NGHE-004.3: Provide visibility into availability performance (uptime/downtime events) to Site Manager and Project Manager during the evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP-NGHE-004.1a: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity during component degradation or failure.
DP-NGHE-004.1b: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable functional units and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed components without system shutdown.
DP-NGHE-004.1c: Establish predictive maintenance and health monitoring framework that detects degradation patterns and triggers preventive intervention before functional failure occurs.

DP-NGHE-004.1d: Implement graceful degradation logic that allows the system to operate at reduced capacity rather than total failure, maintaining partial availability when full capability is unavailable.

DP-NGHE-004.1e: Design self-healing control loops with automated recovery procedures (reset, recalibration, restart sequences) to restore nominal operation from transient faults without human intervention.

DP-NGHE-004.1f: Establish scheduled maintenance windows and operational discipline (spare parts inventory, trained personnel, documented procedures) to minimize unplanned downtime and maximize mean time between failures (MTBF).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-NGHE-004.1: Achieve ≥0.97 availability through a composite strategy combining (1) redundancy and failover for critical subsystems, (2) modular hot-swappable architecture for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive health monitoring with preventive maintenance triggers, and (4) graceful degradation modes that preserve partial availability during subsystem faults. Support with (5) automated self-healing recovery procedures and (6) operational discipline (spare inventory, trained personnel, maintenance scheduling).

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP-004.1a) couples tightly with control architecture (FR-NGHE-002) and power distribution (FR-NGHE-001); failover logic must be designed orthogonally to avoid cascading failures.
Modular hot-swap design (DP-004.1b) couples with interface standardization and mechanical/electrical connector design; poor interface definition will propagate coupling to multiple subsystems.
Predictive maintenance (DP-004.1c) depends on sensor data quality and diagnostic algorithm accuracy; weak sensing (FR-NGHE-003) will degrade availability predictions.
Graceful degradation (DP-004.1d) requires explicit mode-switching logic that must be decoupled from nominal operation paths to avoid introducing new failure modes.
Self-healing recovery (DP-004.1e) may conflict with safety constraints if automated restart sequences are not properly gated by safety interlocks.
coupling_check.notes: The composite DP is not a single orthogonal solution but a portfolio of complementary strategies. Each sub-strategy (redundancy, modularity, monitoring, degradation, recovery) addresses different failure modes and timescales. Coupling risk is moderate if architectural boundaries (module interfaces, failover triggers, safety gates) are explicitly defined and validated. Risk increases if any sub-strategy is omitted without compensating measures.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined target availability (≥0.97) is technically achievable given the system's inherent failure rates and maintenance constraints. RISK: If MTBF or maintenance logistics are incompatible with 0.97 availability, the FR may be infeasible; recommend FMEA and availability modeling early.

ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be designed with independent failure modes (e.g., different vendors, different physical locations, different control algorithms). RISK: Common-cause failures (e.g., shared power supply, environmental stress, software bug in both redundant units) can defeat redundancy; requires explicit common-cause analysis.

ASSUMPTION: Hot-swappable modules can be designed without introducing new failure modes during swap operations (e.g., electrical transients, mechanical misalignment). RISK: Swap procedure itself becomes a failure vector; requires detailed procedure design and operator training.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance data (sensor signals, diagnostic algorithms) are sufficiently accurate to trigger intervention before failure. RISK: False positives (unnecessary maintenance) increase cost; false negatives (missed failures) reduce availability; requires validation against field data.

ASSUMPTION: Graceful degradation modes are acceptable to the customer and do not violate safety or performance contracts. RISK: Reduced-capacity operation may be unsafe or unacceptable in certain worksite contexts; requires explicit customer acceptance of degraded modes.

ASSUMPTION: Operational discipline (spare parts, trained personnel, maintenance scheduling) can be sustained over the evaluation period. RISK: Supply chain disruptions, personnel turnover, or schedule slippage will degrade availability; requires contractual commitment and monitoring.

ASSUMPTION: Safety interlocks and automated recovery procedures do not introduce new hazards or conflict with worksite safety plan. RISK: Automated failover or restart sequences may violate lockout/tagout procedures or create unexpected motion; requires safety review and integration with worksite policies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR-NGHE-004.1 is well-formed and appropriately scoped. Recommend the following refinements to support DP validation and IM design: (1) Decompose the composite DP into explicit sub-DPs for each failure mode category (subsystem redundancy, component replacement, fault detection, mode switching, recovery automation, operational support) with clear ownership and interface boundaries. (2) Define availability measurement methodology (uptime vs. downtime, scheduled vs. unscheduled, partial vs. total failure) to align with customer acceptance criteria. (3) Establish FMEA and availability modeling as early design activities to validate feasibility and identify critical failure modes. (4) Explicitly allocate safety review and worksite policy integration to the IM layer to ensure automated recovery procedures do not conflict with safety interlocks. (5) Define spare parts inventory, maintenance scheduling, and personnel training as contractual obligations with measurable KPIs (e.g., spare availability, technician certification, maintenance compliance rate).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, failover orchestration, health monitoring integration, graceful degradation policies, self-healing procedures, and operational discipline frameworks across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health and status signals from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to feed predictive monitoring and fault detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs failover commands, graceful degradation mode switches, and self-healing recovery procedures to Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits availability-critical alerts and degradation notifications to Safety Services and operational personnel.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports redundancy failover signaling and inter-subsystem coordination for composite availability strategy.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: DP-NGHE-004.1 is fundamentally a system-level availability strategy that orchestrates redundancy, failover, health monitoring, and graceful degradation across all subsystems. NGHE Solution is the natural system-level boundary. Minor diffusion: implementation of subsystem-specific redundancy hardware (e.g., motor redundancy in Vehicle) and subsystem-specific health sensors remain owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation/Safety Services, but coordination logic and policy belong in NGHE Solution.

Poor containment: DP-NGHE-004.1 spans Teleoperation and Safety Services availability as well. Vehicle would only contain its own redundancy and failover logic, forcing critical availability orchestration, cross-subsystem health monitoring, and system-level graceful degradation policies to diffuse across module boundaries. Creates tight coupling and fragmented ownership.

Poor containment: Similar to Vehicle, Teleoperation is a subsystem. DP-NGHE-004.1 requires system-level coordination of availability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Placing it here would fragment the composite strategy and create unmanageable cross-module dependencies.

Poor containment: Safety Services is a subsystem focused on safety logic, not system-level availability orchestration. DP-NGHE-004.1 is broader than safety and requires coordination across all subsystems. High diffusion and misalignment with Safety Services' primary responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP-NGHE-004.1 is a system-level availability architecture that inherently requires cross-subsystem coordination, unified health monitoring, centralized failover orchestration, and system-wide graceful degradation policies. NGHE Solution is the only module boundary broad enough to contain this composite strategy without major diffusion. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement subsystem-specific redundancy and health sensors, but NGHE Solution owns the integration, orchestration, and operational discipline framework.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it accumulates too much operational logic. Mitigation: Clearly separate policy (owned by NGHE Solution) from subsystem-specific implementation (owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Use well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) to enforce boundaries.

Risk: Operational discipline (spare inventory, trained personnel, maintenance scheduling) is organizational, not purely technical. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the technical framework (predictive monitoring triggers, maintenance scheduling algorithms); operational execution is a separate concern managed by operations teams.

Risk: Self-healing recovery procedures may require subsystem-specific knowledge. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the orchestration logic and decision trees; subsystems implement subsystem-specific recovery actions triggered by NGHE Solution commands via Tasking interface.

Risk: Predictive health monitoring may require real-time processing that conflicts with other NGHE Solution responsibilities. Mitigation: Consider a dedicated health monitoring service or agent within NGHE Solution boundary, or delegate sensor-level processing to subsystems with aggregation at NGHE Solution level.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability target (≥0.97) is measurable and verifiable over the agreed evaluation period. Acceptance criteria are quantitative and testable. However, verification requires instrumentation across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) to feed telemetry into NGHE Solution's health monitoring. Feasibility is high if telemetry interfaces are properly implemented and data collection is continuous.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-004.1 is clearly allocated to NGHE Solution as the system-level availability orchestrator. Subsystem-specific redundancy and health sensors are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively. Allocation is clear, but requires explicit interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) to enforce boundaries and prevent creep.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-NGHE-004.1 directly addresses FR-NGHE-004.1 through a composite strategy: (1) redundancy and failover, (2) modular hot-swappable architecture, (3) predictive health monitoring, (4) graceful degradation, (5) self-healing recovery, and (6) operational discipline. The DP is complete, logically sound, and measurable against the ≥0.97 availability target. No logical gaps detected between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Predictive health monitoring logic may diffuse across subsystems if sensor-level processing is not clearly separated from aggregation/decision logic. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns decision trees and trigger thresholds; subsystems own sensor implementations and local preprocessing.

HIGH: Self-healing recovery procedures risk fragmentation if subsystem-specific recovery actions are not centrally orchestrated. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns orchestration state machine and command sequencing via Tasking interface; subsystems implement recovery actions as atomic, well-defined operations.

MEDIUM: Graceful degradation policies may scatter across subsystems if mode-switching logic is not centralized. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns degradation policy matrix and mode-switch decisions; subsystems implement mode-specific behavior in response to Tasking commands.

MEDIUM: Operational discipline (spare inventory, maintenance scheduling) is organizational and may not map cleanly to a technical module. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns the technical framework (predictive maintenance triggers, scheduling algorithms); operational execution is external to the module boundary but coordinated via SafetyAlert interface.

LOW: Redundancy and failover coordination across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services may create tight coupling if not mediated by well-defined interfaces. Mitigation: Network Link interface must support asynchronous, loosely-coupled failover signaling to prevent cascading failures.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: DP-NGHE-004.1 achieves 0.92 containment score within NGHE Solution boundary. This is appropriate for a system-level availability architecture. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and expected: subsystem-specific redundancy hardware and health sensors remain owned by Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, but coordination logic, policy, and orchestration are properly centralized in NGHE Solution. The four external interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) provide clear boundaries and enforce separation of concerns. Risk of NGHE Solution becoming a 'god module' is mitigated by explicit interface contracts and clear delegation of subsystem-specific implementation to subsystems.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1: Explicitly decompose DP-NGHE-004.1 into sub-DPs for each component: (1a) DP-NGHE-004.1.1 for redundancy and failover orchestration, (1b) DP-NGHE-004.1.2 for modular hot-swappable architecture (design principle, not implementation), (1c) DP-NGHE-004.1.3 for predictive health monitoring aggregation and decision logic, (1d) DP-NGHE-004.1.4 for graceful degradation policy matrix, (1e) DP-NGHE-004.1.5 for self-healing recovery orchestration, (1f) DP-NGHE-004.1.6 for operational discipline framework. This clarifies ownership and reduces ambiguity.

REVISION 2: Formalize the Telemetry interface contract to specify: (a) required health signals from each subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), (b) sampling rates and latency requirements, (c) data schema and encoding, (d) failure modes (e.g., sensor loss, network delay). This prevents diffusion of health monitoring logic across subsystems.

REVISION 3: Formalize the Tasking interface contract to specify: (a) failover commands (e.g., 'switch to redundant motor'), (b) graceful degradation mode switches (e.g., 'enter reduced-speed mode'), (c) self-healing recovery procedures (e.g., 'restart subsystem X'), (d) command sequencing and atomicity guarantees, (e) acknowledgment and status feedback. This ensures subsystems implement recovery actions consistently.

REVISION 4: Clarify the boundary between NGHE Solution's technical framework and operational execution. NGHE Solution owns: predictive maintenance trigger algorithms, maintenance scheduling logic, degradation policy matrices, failover decision trees. Operations teams own: spare inventory management, personnel training, actual maintenance execution. SafetyAlert interface should emit maintenance recommendations, not execute them.

REVISION 5: Consider a dedicated health monitoring service or agent within NGHE Solution boundary to handle real-time telemetry aggregation, anomaly detection, and predictive analysis. This prevents health monitoring logic from diffusing into other NGHE Solution responsibilities (failover orchestration, graceful degradation, self-healing recovery).

REVISION 6: Add acceptance criteria for interface latency and reliability. For example: 'Failover command latency ≤ 100ms', 'Telemetry ingestion reliability ≥ 0.99', 'SafetyAlert delivery reliability ≥ 0.99'. This ensures the composite availability strategy is not undermined by interface failures.

REVISION 7: Explicitly address the 'modular hot-swappable architecture' component of DP-NGHE-004.1. This is a design principle that should be enforced at the subsystem level (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) through interface contracts and mechanical/electrical standards. NGHE Solution should assume subsystems comply with this principle but not own its implementation.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is clear, measurable, and properly allocated. The DP is logically sound and complete. The DP-IM gate identifies manageable diffusion risks with clear mitigation strategies and a healthy 0.92 containment score. However, the recommended revisions—particularly REVISION 1 (decompose DP into sub-DPs for each component) and REVISION 5 (dedicated health monitoring service)—indicate that the current DP-NGHE-004.1 is composite and would benefit from explicit sub-level decomposition to clarify ownership, reduce ambiguity, and enforce interface contracts. Given the high safety criticality of this availability requirement, deriving next-level sub-FRs is justified to ensure each component (redundancy orchestration, health monitoring, graceful degradation, self-healing recovery, operational discipline) is independently verifiable and traceable. This also supports the architectural goal of preventing NGHE Solution from becoming a 'god module' by explicitly packaging each concern as a distinct sub-FR/DP/IM triplet.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1.1: Orchestrate failover and redundancy activation across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems to ensure seamless transition to backup resources within 100ms upon primary resource failure.

FR-NGHE-004.1.2: Aggregate real-time health telemetry from all subsystems, detect anomalies, and predict imminent failures using statistical and machine-learning models to enable proactive maintenance and preventive failover.

FR-NGHE-004.1.3: Enforce graceful degradation policies by dynamically switching system operating modes (e.g., full autonomy → reduced-speed teleoperation → safe stop) based on subsystem health state and mission constraints.

FR-NGHE-004.1.4: Orchestrate self-healing recovery procedures by sequencing atomic recovery actions (e.g., subsystem restart, sensor recalibration, network reconnection) across subsystems with rollback capability on failure.

FR-NGHE-004.1.5: Maintain and communicate operational discipline framework (predictive maintenance triggers, scheduling algorithms, spare inventory recommendations) to operations teams via SafetyAlert interface to sustain long-term availability.

FR-NGHE-004.1.6: Enforce modular hot-swappable architecture design principle across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services through interface contracts, mechanical/electrical standards, and compliance verification.

revise.why: Before decomposing to sub-FRs, apply REVISION 1 and REVISION 5 from the gate output: (1) Explicitly decompose DP-NGHE-004.1 into six sub-DPs corresponding to the six sub-FRs above, each with clear ownership and interface contracts. (2) Introduce a dedicated Health Monitoring Service (or agent) within NGHE Solution boundary to handle telemetry aggregation, anomaly detection, and predictive analysis, preventing diffusion of health monitoring logic into failover orchestration, graceful degradation, or self-healing recovery. (3) Formalize Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interface contracts (REVISIONS 2, 3, 4, 6) to specify data schemas, latency/reliability requirements, command semantics, and failure modes. This ensures sub-level decomposition is grounded in well-defined interfaces and reduces ambiguity during implementation.

assumptions: Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) implement health sensors and local preprocessing; NGHE Solution owns aggregation, decision logic, and orchestration.
Failover latency requirement of 100ms is achievable with asynchronous, loosely-coupled signaling via Network Link interface.
Telemetry ingestion and SafetyAlert delivery reliability targets (≥0.99) are achievable with redundant communication paths and buffering.
Operational discipline (spare inventory, maintenance scheduling) is coordinated by NGHE Solution's technical framework but executed by external operations teams.
Modular hot-swappable architecture is a design principle enforced at subsystem level; NGHE Solution assumes compliance but does not own implementation.
Predictive health monitoring uses statistical and machine-learning models; model accuracy and false-positive rates are acceptable for the ≥0.97 availability target.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Telemetry interface latency or packet loss could undermine health monitoring accuracy and failover decision quality. Mitigation: Formalize Telemetry interface contract with latency and reliability SLAs; implement buffering and anomaly detection for missing data.

CRITICAL: Failover orchestration logic may become a bottleneck or single point of failure if not properly distributed or replicated. Mitigation: Design failover orchestration as a stateless service with multiple instances or as a distributed consensus protocol.

HIGH: Self-healing recovery procedures may fail or cascade if subsystem-specific recovery actions are not atomic and well-tested. Mitigation: Define recovery actions as atomic operations with explicit preconditions and postconditions; implement rollback logic.

HIGH: Graceful degradation policy matrix may become inconsistent or incomplete if not centrally maintained and versioned. Mitigation: Treat policy matrix as a configuration artifact with version control, review process, and automated validation.

MEDIUM: Health monitoring models (statistical, ML-based) may have high false-positive rates, triggering unnecessary failovers or maintenance. Mitigation: Implement model validation and tuning process; set conservative thresholds initially and refine based on operational data.

MEDIUM: Interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) may not be enforced at runtime, leading to integration failures. Mitigation: Implement interface validation and contract testing; use schema validation and protocol checkers.
evidence_needed: Formalized Telemetry interface contract specifying health signals, sampling rates, data schema, and failure modes for each subsystem.
Formalized Tasking interface contract specifying failover commands, graceful degradation mode switches, recovery procedures, and acknowledgment semantics.
Formalized SafetyAlert interface contract specifying maintenance recommendations, alert severity levels, and delivery guarantees.
Formalized Network Link interface contract specifying latency, reliability, and failure modes for asynchronous failover signaling.
Design specification for Health Monitoring Service (or agent) including telemetry aggregation, anomaly detection algorithms, predictive models, and decision logic.
Design specification for Failover Orchestration Service including state machine, command sequencing, rollback logic, and latency guarantees.
Design specification for Graceful Degradation Policy Engine including policy matrix, mode-switch logic, and consistency validation.
Design specification for Self-Healing Recovery Orchestrator including recovery action sequencing, atomicity guarantees, and rollback procedures.
Operational discipline framework specification including predictive maintenance trigger algorithms, scheduling logic, and spare inventory recommendations.
Acceptance criteria for interface latency (e.g., failover command latency ≤100ms, telemetry ingestion latency ≤500ms) and reliability (e.g., telemetry ingestion ≥0.99, SafetyAlert delivery ≥0.99).
Test plan for failover scenarios, graceful degradation transitions, self-healing recovery procedures, and interface contract compliance.
Verification plan for ≥0.97 availability target including instrumentation, telemetry collection, and evaluation methodology over agreed evaluation period.
